title: McIlroy Does It Again: Back-to-Back Masters at Augusta National
excerpt: Rory McIlroy claimed his second consecutive Masters title with a one-shot victory over Scottie Scheffler, joining Tiger Woods as only the fourth back-to-back champion in Augusta National history.
body:
McIlroy's Amen Corner Masterclass<\/h2>

Rory McIlroy seized his place in Augusta National history on Sunday, claiming his second consecutive Masters with a composed 1-under 71 to finish at 12-under 276. One shot ahead of Scottie Scheffler, McIlroy pulled away decisively around Amen Corner with a pair of immaculate birdies — a fearless 9-iron to 7 feet on the par-3 12th, then a sublime 350-yard drive on the par-5 13th setting up another birdie to establish a three-shot lead. With back-to-back green jackets now in his cabinet, the Northern Irishman joins Tiger Woods, Nick Faldo, and Jack Nicklaus in rarefied air, and sits just one major away from tying Harry Vardon's all-time record of seven majors.<\/p>

Winning Score<\/div>
12-under<\/div><\/div>
Margin of Victory<\/div>
1 shot<\/div><\/div>
Major Titles<\/div>
6<\/div><\/div>
Back-to-Back Masters<\/div>
4th Ever<\/div><\/div><\/div>

The Plot Thickens Down the Stretch<\/h2>

This was no coronation. McIlroy had to earn every shot of his second green jacket. Coming into Sunday, three players held a share of the lead — and the Masters had every hallmark of a dramatic finish. Justin Rose, at 45, was chasing what would have been the second-oldest Masters victory since Jack Nicklaus in 1986. He stormed the front nine with three straight birdies to seize the lead. But Amen Corner, golf's most fearsome stretch, made him pay. Two bogeys and a missed 8-foot birdie putt on the 13th — where an eagle attempt ran 8 feet by — cost him dearly. Cameron Young, who'd led by two, surrendered his advantage just as quickly with three-putt bogeys and poor club selection around the turn. McIlroy, meanwhile, thrived when it mattered most.<\/p>

Scheffler's Valiant Comeback Falls Just Short<\/h2>

Scottie Scheffler, the world's No. 1 player, arrived at Augusta 12 shots behind after 36 holes and nearly pulled off the greatest comeback in Masters history. His weekend was virtually flawless — a bogey-free 65-68 made him the first player since 1942 to go clean on both weekend rounds. But on a Sunday with accessible pins designed for low scoring, 11 straight pars from Scheffler simply weren't enough. He finished just one shot back, his third runner-up finish in the majors to go with four titles. A near-miss that will sting, but a display of resilience that reasserted his credentials as the tour's best player.<\/p>

The Real McIlroy Emerges<\/h2>

"I just can't believe I waited 17 years to get one green jacket, and I get two in a row," McIlroy said in the moments after his victory. A year ago, he'd broken his Masters curse with a playoff win over Justin Rose. This time, there was no relief — only joy. He endured a chaotic finish (wild tee shot on 18, bogey) with the same equanimity he showed all week, cementing a legacy at Augusta that once seemed destined to haunt him forever. The biggest post-Masters question now: can McIlroy make it three in a row in 2027? Only time will tell, but after this weekend, no one would bet against the Northern Irishman.<\/p>
